summarization.a: Mass spectrum (m/z 131–140) recorded during the first HF exposure at 150 °C, highlighting boroxine-derived fluorinated and hydroxylated boron oxide species. Overlaid schematic structures indicate the proposed chemical assignments of the dominant ion groups, showing coexistence of fully fluorinated and partially hydroxylated boroxine motifs during the initial etching stage.

data_extraction.a:
| m/z   | Intensity (mV) |  Boroxine Ring Species | 
|-------|----------------|------------------------|  
| 133.2 | ~0.20          | B3O2F(OH)2 Fragment (Green)|  
| 134.2 | ~0.45          | B3O2F(OH)2 Fragment (Green/Blue)|  
| 135.2 | ~0.46          | B3O2F2(OH) Fragment (Blue)|  
| 136.2 | ~0.70          | Main B3O2F2(OH) Peak (BLue/Red)|  
| 137.2 | ~0.32          | B3O3F3 Fragment (Red)|  
| 138.2 | ~0.32          | B3O3F3 Fragment (Red)|

a.question: What does the simultaneous presence of fluorinated and hydroxylated boroxine species indicate about the surface chemistry during the first HF exposure?
a.answer: It indicates incomplete fluorination during the initial reaction stage, where residual hydroxyl groups remain on boroxine units while fluorination proceeds concurrently. This reflects a transient, non-steady-state surface composition during the first HF pulse. Such coexistence is expected before the surface becomes uniformly fluorinated in later cycles.

a.question: Are fully fluorinated boroxine species more intense than hydroxylated ones in this spectrum?
a.answer: No, hydroxylated boroxine species (specifically at m/z 136) show significantly higher intensity (~0.70 mV) than the fully fluorinated species (m/z 137–138) in this spectrum

a.question: Why do multiple closely spaced peaks appear in the m/z 131–140 region for boroxine-related ions?
a.answer: Due to isotopic contributions from 10B/11B and fluorine, combined with fragmentation of closely related boroxine-derived species.

a.question: Does this spectrum correspond to a steady-state etching condition?
